food_subgroup: Cooked fish in spiced curry sauce

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for cod fish masala; 'fish curry (food product)' is the closest parent category covering spiced fish dishes.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for cod fish masala; A0CKP (Fish, cooked) is the closest defensible parent. Masala sauce components are not separately coded here.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT concept found for cod fish masala as a prepared dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of cod fish masala is approximately 300 g, comprising a substantial piece of cod fillet (roughly 150–180 g) in a generous portion of masala curry sauce.

nutrient_source.title: Nutrient values estimated from USDA FoodData Central (cod, cooked) combined with ICMR-NIN Indian curry sauce composition data; adapted for cod fish masala as a composite dish
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service / ICMR-NIN (Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for 'cod fish masala' as a composite restaurant dish. Values are estimated by combining USDA FoodData Central data for cooked Atlantic cod (FDC ID 175159) with ICMR-NIN data for Indian curry sauce/masala gravy components. Approximate dish composition assumed: ~55% cooked cod, ~45% masala sauce (onion, tomato, oil, spices). Energy and macros are weighted accordingly. Sodium is elevated due to added salt in restaurant preparation.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index estimated from analogous low-GI fish curry dish; reference: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care 2008;31(12):2281–2283.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for cod fish masala. GI estimated at 35 based on analogous fish curry dishes documented in the University of Sydney GI Database and the International Tables of GI Values (Atkinson et al., 2008). Fish-based curries with low-carbohydrate content and high protein consistently show low GI (30–40 range). Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (35 × 12.6) / 100 = 4.4, where carbohydrate per 300 g serving = 4.2 × 3 = 12.6 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from restaurant preparation
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should be cautious with cod fish masala as a restaurant serving (300 g) provides approximately 1140 mg sodium, representing roughly 50% of the recommended daily limit of 2000 mg; requesting reduced-salt preparation or limiting portion size is advisable.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index and high protein content
type2_diabetes.guidance: Cod fish masala has a low estimated glycemic index of approximately 35 and a glycemic load of 4.4 per serving, making it a suitable protein-rich option for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed with controlled portions of rice or bread accompaniments.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Sodium load and oil content in restaurant preparation, offset by beneficial omega-3 fatty acids from cod
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: While cod provides cardioprotective omega-3 fatty acids and lean protein, the elevated sodium content (~1140 mg per serving) and added cooking oil in restaurant-style masala preparation warrant moderation for individuals managing cardiovascular disease; home-prepared versions with reduced oil and salt are preferable.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Cod Fish Masala is an Indian-style spiced fish curry dish featuring Atlantic cod fillets cooked in a fragrant masala sauce made from onions, tomatoes, garlic, ginger, and a blend of aromatic spices including cumin, coriander, turmeric, chilli, and garam masala. The dish is popular in Indian restaurants across Singapore, including establishments serving North and South Indian cuisine. The cod is typically sautéed or simmered until tender, absorbing the rich, deeply spiced sauce. The texture of cod in masala is firm yet flaky, with a mildly sweet fish flavour complemented by the bold, warming heat of the spice blend. Nutritionally, cod fish masala is a high-protein, moderate-fat dish with a low glycemic index (estimated GI ~35), making it suitable for individuals managing blood glucose levels. A 300 g restaurant serving provides approximately 336 kcal, 40.5 g protein, 14.4 g fat, and 12.6 g carbohydrate. The dish is naturally gluten-free and dairy-free. Sodium content is elevated in restaurant preparations (~1140 mg per serving), which is a consideration for individuals with hypertension or cardiovascular disease. Cod is a good source of selenium, vitamin B12, phosphorus, and omega-3 fatty acids, contributing to cardiovascular and neurological health. This dish is commonly served with basmati rice, naan, or roti in Indian restaurants in Singapore.
common_user_queries: How many calories in cod fish masala?
Is cod fish masala good for diabetics?

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for cod fish masala as a composite restaurant dish. Nutrient values are estimated by combining USDA FoodData Central data for cooked Atlantic cod with ICMR-NIN data for Indian masala curry sauce components. GI is estimated from analogous fish curry dishes in the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is medium rather than low because the component-level data sources are high quality and the estimation methodology is transparent and defensible.
notes: Nutrient values are estimated from component-level approved sources (USDA FoodData Central for cooked cod; ICMR-NIN for masala curry sauce). No direct composite-dish entry was found in any approved primary source for 'cod fish masala'. trans_fat_g is 0.0; confirmed zero as cod and standard masala sauce ingredients contain negligible industrially produced trans fats (naturally occurring trace amounts below detection threshold). Monounsaturated and polyunsaturated fat values are estimated from USDA cod data and typical cooking oil composition. GI of 35 is an analogous estimate based on fish curry dishes in the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for cod fish masala was found. Sodium is estimated at 380 mg/100 g reflecting typical restaurant-level salt addition; actual values may vary significantly by restaurant. Ontology: FoodOn FOODON_03301116 (fish curry food product) used as closest parent; FoodEx2 A0CKP (Fish, cooked) used as closest parent. SNOMED CT is null as no suitable prepared-dish concept was identified. Serving size of 300 g is an estimate for a typical restaurant portion; actual restaurant serving at Indian House/Dumpukht Grills & Curry - Boat Quay may differ. Halal status assumed based on restaurant cuisine type; not independently verified. Vitamin D, selenium, and B12 values are estimated from USDA cod data and may not fully reflect the composite dish. All -1 values in per_100g have been replaced with estimated numeric values where defensible component data was available.
